sections.content:
Interesting - the white diamond is now at (32, 32) and the orange ring has moved from (12, 24) to (6, 17)! This is a different state. The orange ring is now at the starting position of the white diamond.
Looking at the image, I can see the orange ring has moved to where the white diamond started. The white diamond is at the center (32, 32).
This is a new state. Let me check if the dark gray diamonds are still in the same positions and try to understand the new connectivity.
Let me check the current positions of all key objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("Current state:")
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] < 30 and n['color'] in ['W', 'G', 'p', 'O', 'M']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, center={center}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Dark gray diamonds at (6, 17), (21, 8), (35, 45), (48, 54) - same positions

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White diamond at (32,32). Try clicking a dark gray diamond
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 48, 'col': 54}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
w_pos = None
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        w_pos = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
print(f"White diamond at: {w_pos}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
